/.title: Nutanix Monitoring & Observability | Overwatch
h1s:
Overwatch keeps your Nutanix stack
in one operational view.

headings.text:
Overwatch keeps your Nutanix stack
in one operational view.
Nutanix is the anchor. Everything around it stops feeling disconnected.
Five engines. One console. Here's what's inside.
Replace console sprawl without replacing the tools your team already trusts.
Built by engineers who spent a decade managing Nutanix at scale.
See what your infrastructure looks like in one console.

paras: One appliance. Full-stack visibility. Overwatch monitors your Nutanix clusters, bare-metal servers, network devices, and backups with ML-driven anomaly detection and optional Datadog or Grafana forwarding.

Overwatch starts where most teams actually feel the pain: Prism is critical, but hardware, networking, backup posture, and storage dependencies still live somewhere else. The product is designed to keep Nutanix at the center while pulling the surrounding operational evidence into one console.

Overwatch is not a pretty dashboard sitting on top of disconnected data. It is a local operations stack that collects, enriches, analyzes, stores, and publishes infrastructure signals from one place. That is how the product reduces console sprawl instead of adding another screen to check.

Overwatch runs as a local processing and storage layer that consolidates Nutanix, backup, storage, hardware, network, and flow evidence before optionally forwarding selected data to the cloud platforms your team already uses. Local first. Cloud when you choose it.
Keep telemetry local unless you explicitly enable forwarding.
Filter what leaves the appliance so cloud analytics costs stay predictable.
Use one local control plane instead of piling new dashboards on top of old ones.
Cluster health, inventory, protection state, tasks, and multi-cluster context from Prism Element and Prism Central.
HYCU, object storage, retention posture, and target utilization in the same operator view.
Redfish, IPMI, SNMP, IPFIX, and NetFlow signals tied back to the workloads they actually affect.
Local collection, enrichment, analysis, dashboards, reports, and APIs stay inside one controlled platform boundary.

Logic Insight builds Overwatch because the operating model around enterprise infrastructure got worse, not better. Nutanix teams got one console. Backup teams kept another. Storage teams had their own. Networking lived somewhere else. Hardware health and flow telemetry each added another tab, another dashboard, another handoff.

Overwatch starts with deep Nutanix coverage, then extends that same operating model across bare-metal servers, SNMP devices, protection posture, storage context, and network flows inside one local Ubuntu appliance. It stores telemetry on-prem by default, uses open components like VictoriaMetrics, VictoriaLogs, Grafana, and a secured API surface, and only forwards data to Datadog, Grafana Cloud, or Coralogix when you explicitly want it to.
Explore how Overwatch brings Nutanix, backups, cloud storage, hardware, networking, and flow telemetry into one operating surface without forcing a cloud-first control plane.

/about/.title: About the Team | Logic Insight
h1s: We got tired of the same monitoring problems you have.

headings.text: We got tired of the same monitoring problems you have.
Five consoles for one environment felt broken. So we fixed it.
The company is small on purpose. The experience behind it is not.
If you're trying to reduce console sprawl across Nutanix, backups, storage, and network operations — we should talk.

paras: Logic Insight was founded in 2025 by infrastructure engineers who spent over a decade managing Nutanix environments — and got frustrated opening five consoles just to troubleshoot one issue. So we built the tool we wished existed.

Every Nutanix admin knows the drill. An alert fires, and suddenly you're bouncing between Prism Central, your backup console, a hardware management interface, a network dashboard, and whatever you're using for storage monitoring. Five tabs. Five logins. Five different views of the same environment.

We lived that for years across enterprise environments — healthcare, financial services, managed service providers. The tools existed, but none of them talked to each other, and none of them were built for the way Nutanix actually works.

Overwatch is the result. A single on-prem appliance that collects Nutanix, hardware, backup, network, and storage telemetry into one operational model. No cloud dependency. No per-metric billing. 50+ pre-built dashboards and ML-driven baselines that learn what normal looks like in your environment.
CO-FOUNDER & PRINCIPAL PLATFORM ARCHITECT

David has spent over 15 years in enterprise infrastructure — from NetApp storage and VMware environments to Nutanix HCI at scale. He has worked across managed service providers, Caribbean operations, and private cloud deployments for mid-market and enterprise organizations. He holds 10+ Nutanix certifications spanning AI, Unified Storage, Multicloud Infrastructure, and Services, along with NVIDIA AI certifications. He designed and built the Overwatch platform from the ground up.
CO-FOUNDER & CEO/COO

Jason brings over 20 years of experience scaling and operating businesses, with $60M+ in cumulative business growth and successful exits. He holds a Bachelor's degree in Business, Management, and Marketing from the University of Florida. At Logic Insight, he leads go-to-market strategy, partnerships, and operations — making sure the product David builds actually reaches the teams that need it.

/ahv-monitoring/.title: AHV Monitoring | Logic Insight
h1s: AHV Monitoring

headings.text: How AHV monitoring differs from ESXi monitoring
Why teams care about AHV monitoring
How it fits into the broader cluster view

paras: Host, VM, and hypervisor visibility without agent sprawl

Overwatch gives Nutanix teams deep AHV monitoring across hosts, VMs, storage behavior, and infrastructure trends. See the hypervisor layer clearly, correlate it with the rest of the stack, and send clean signals to Datadog or Grafana.
AHV monitoring should help teams understand why workloads are behaving the way they are, not just show another CPU chart.
Track AHV host utilization, CVM behavior, and hypervisor health without reducing everything to generic VM counters.
Understand resource pressure, noisy neighbors, and emerging performance risks at the VM layer.
Correlate VM performance with storage latency, capacity posture, and underlying infrastructure behavior.
Tie AHV signals back to Prism Central, network flow, and physical hardware so teams can isolate root cause faster.
AHV monitoring needs to show what is happening at the host, VM, and hypervisor layers without losing the CVM context that makes Nutanix different from generic virtualization stacks.

Teams moving from VMware usually discover that the old monitoring assumptions do not map cleanly onto AHV. The API, resource model, and scheduling behavior are different, so the monitoring path has to change too.

That is why AHV monitoring usually breaks for VMware migrations when teams try to reuse the old observability path. The objects, APIs, and scheduling assumptions changed. Overwatch speaks Prism Element natively so the collection layer matches the platform instead of forcing AHV through a VMware-shaped adapter.
AHV is where resource contention, workload behavior, and infrastructure drift become operationally visible. The challenge is seeing those signals with enough Nutanix context to act quickly.

Every AHV host runs a CVM. Overwatch correlates CVM resource consumption with workload VM performance on the same host, so when VMs show elevated storage latency or I/O stalls the operator can immediately ask the right host-level questions: is the CVM under CPU pressure, is memory allocation below recommended, is the oplog filling, and is the storage path on that node falling behind?

That removes the manual cross-referencing teams do today between Prism’s VM view, host view, and storage view. The host, its CVM, and the workload symptoms stay in one operational thread instead of three separate tabs.

In practice that means a VM complaint stops being “storage feels slow somewhere.” The operator can see whether one host is carrying abnormal CVM load, whether balloon pressure is concentrated on a subset of workloads, and whether the affected VMs all land on the same physical node. That host-scoped correlation is what shortens AHV investigations.

AHV monitoring is stronger when it is not isolated. Overwatch connects host and VM behavior back to Prism management signals, storage posture, and network flow analysis so the operator does not have to infer root cause from one chart at a time.
Deploy Overwatch and get per-host, per-VM, and CVM-correlated AHV visibility alongside hardware health, network flows, and backup status.
items: • CPU utilization at aggregate and per-core levels so hot cores do not hide behind host averages.
• Memory utilization with balloon driver state so operators can tell healthy efficiency from real pressure.
• NIC throughput, packet rates, and error counts for each host-level data path.
• CVM resource allocation versus actual CPU and memory consumption on every host.
• vCPU utilization and CPU ready time so waiting-for-core behavior is visible before users complain.
• Memory assigned versus memory reclaimed by the balloon driver for each workload.
• vDisk IOPS and latency, split into read and write paths for troubleshooting.
• vNIC throughput per virtual adapter plus snapshot count and total snapshot size.
• ADS migration events so teams know when and why a VM moved.
• Host maintenance mode entry and exit to keep operational changes in the timeline.
• HA reservation status changes that affect failover posture and cluster headroom.
• Capacity planning for expanding Nutanix estates
• Investigating noisy-neighbor behavior and VM hotspots
• Correlating VM symptoms with host, storage, and network causes
• Feeding Nutanix-aware telemetry into Datadog or Grafana dashboards

AHV metrics Overwatch collects

AHV monitoring needs to show what is happening at the host, VM, and hypervisor layers without losing the CVM context that makes Nutanix different from generic virtualization stacks.

Per-host metrics
• CPU utilization at aggregate and per-core levels so hot cores do not hide behind host averages.

How AHV monitoring differs from ESXi monitoring

Teams moving from VMware usually discover that the old monitoring assumptions do not map cleanly onto AHV. The API, resource model, and scheduling behavior are different, so the monitoring path has to change too.

AHV exposes its telemetry through Prism APIs, not the vSphere SDK or ESXi SNMP patterns VMware teams are used to.
Balloon memory and CPU ready time are surfaced differently, so old VMware dashboards and alert rules rarely carry over cleanly.
There is no DRS equivalent that behaves exactly like VMware. ADS makes placement decisions less frequently and with different operating assumptions.
Overwatch collects from the Prism Element API on each cluster directly instead of adapting AHV through a generic VM metrics bridge.

That is why AHV monitoring usually breaks for VMware migrations when teams try to reuse the old observability path. The objects, APIs, and scheduling assumptions changed. Overwatch speaks Prism Element natively so the collection layer matches the platform instead of forcing AHV through a VMware-shaped adapter.

Why teams care about AHV monitoring

AHV is where resource contention, workload behavior, and infrastructure drift become operationally visible. The challenge is seeing those signals with enough Nutanix context to act quickly.

• Capacity planning for expanding Nutanix estates
• Investigating noisy-neighbor behavior and VM hotspots
• Correlating VM symptoms with host, storage, and network causes
• Feeding Nutanix-aware telemetry into Datadog or Grafana dashboards
CVM-to-host correlation

Every AHV host runs a CVM. Overwatch correlates CVM resource consumption with workload VM performance on the same host, so when VMs show elevated storage latency or I/O stalls the operator can immediately ask the right host-level questions: is the CVM under CPU pressure, is memory allocation below recommended, is the oplog filling, and is the storage path on that node falling behind?

That removes the manual cross-referencing teams do today between Prism’s VM view, host view, and storage view. The host, its CVM, and the workload symptoms stay in one operational thread instead of three separate tabs.

In practice that means a VM complaint stops being “storage feels slow somewhere.” The operator can see whether one host is carrying abnormal CVM load, whether balloon pressure is concentrated on a subset of workloads, and whether the affected VMs all land on the same physical node. That host-scoped correlation is what shortens AHV investigations.

How it fits into the broader cluster view

AHV monitoring is stronger when it is not isolated. Overwatch connects host and VM behavior back to Prism management signals, storage posture, and network flow analysis so the operator does not have to infer root cause from one chart at a time.

h1s: One platform boundary for Nutanix and everything around it.

headings.text: One platform boundary for Nutanix and everything around it.
How Overwatch turns signals into one operational console
The product is opinionated because operations work is messy enough already.
See what your infrastructure looks like in one console.

paras: Overwatch is architected as a self-contained on-prem operations platform. Nutanix is still the center of gravity, but the platform is designed to reduce console sprawl around it by pulling hardware, network, backup, and flow context into the same operating surface.
Telemetry stays local unless you explicitly configure an output destination.
Internal services stay behind one controlled HTTPS entry point.
Operators get one appliance to manage instead of a stack of sidecar tooling.
Prism Element & Central, Redfish, IPMI, SNMP, HYCU, NetFlow
Unified ingestion fabric normalizes all telemetry sources
Baselines, anomaly detection, health scoring, forecasting
VictoriaMetrics & VictoriaLogs with local-first retention
Pre-built views, scheduled reports, optional forwarding
Each stage in the appliance is designed to reduce handoffs: collect once, enrich locally, analyze with context, then decide what stays on-prem and what gets forwarded.
Prism Element, Prism Central, Redfish, IPMI, SNMP, HYCU, IPFIX, and NetFlow all enter through one appliance boundary instead of separate collectors and consoles.
Metrics, logs, dashboards, APIs, and reports live inside one local stack, so teams stop stitching context back together by hand.
Overwatch applies seasonal baselines, anomaly detection, health scoring, and forecasting across infrastructure domains, not just within one data source.
Operators can work from the local dashboards and reports on the appliance, then forward filtered data to Datadog, Grafana Cloud, or Coralogix if needed.
The appliance is designed for on-prem data sovereignty first, with optional managed assistance around it if a team wants help operating the platform.
Explore how one local platform can replace console sprawl without forcing your team into a new cloud control plane.

Overwatch was built for Nutanix — not adapted from a generic tool.

We don't scrape SNMP or install agents. Overwatch speaks Prism Central and Prism Element API natively — accessing data that generic tools can't reach. Categories, protection policies, storage containers, CVM metrics — all first-class data.

When a VM is slow, is it the VM, the host, the storage, or the hardware? Overwatch correlates metrics across all layers automatically. A disk latency spike on a VM traces back to a hot vDisk on a storage tier on a specific controller — in one view.

Run local dashboards and scheduled reports from the appliance, or selectively forward filtered telemetry to external tools.
Reduce the number of consoles teams live in without giving up the systems they still need downstream.

REST endpoints for reporting, integration, and downstream automation.

In three-tier environments, teams could monitor compute, storage, and network independently. Different teams used different tools and different dashboards. Generic monitoring tools still treat those as disconnected data streams. They collect CPU metrics, storage metrics, and network metrics independently, then display them in separate dashboards. The correlation that matters, the CVM on node 3 is CPU-starved, which is causing storage latency for 12 VMs, which is saturating the replication network, still has to be reconstructed by a human.

3.Send only the data streams you actually want in Datadog to control cost and reduce noise.
4.Use Datadog for monitors, workflows, and cross-platform dashboards while keeping the appliance as the local collection and context layer.
• Relying on generic polling and losing cluster-specific context
• Forwarding too much raw telemetry into Datadog and driving up spend

/network-flow-analysis/.title: Nutanix Network Monitoring with IPFIX & NetFlow
h1s: Nutanix Network Monitoring and Flow Analysis

headings.text: Nutanix Network Monitoring and Flow Analysis
What You See With Flow Analysis
How Flow Data Gets to Your Dashboards
Network Flow as a Security Signal
How to Use IPFIX to Map East-West Nutanix Traffic
Why IPFIX Changes Nutanix Root-Cause Analysis
Redfish and SNMP Together: Why Hardware and Network Context Both Matter
See Your Network In a New Light

paras: See Every East-West Conversation Across Your Nutanix Environment

Overwatch extends Nutanix monitoring into the network layer by collecting and analyzing IPFIX flow data, giving you service-centric traffic visualization, DNS monitoring, region-aware mapping, and deep visibility into who is talking to whom, why the traffic exists, and where bottlenecks are developing.

Packet counters tell you nothing about what's happening on your network. Overwatch shows you the actual conversations — which VMs are talking to which services, over which ports, and how traffic patterns change over time.

Visualize your entire network topology as an interactive constellation map. Every VM, every IP, every connection — color-coded by region, sized by traffic volume, and filterable by service, protocol, or time range.
See traffic not just by IP, but by service. Overwatch maps flows to their actual services — DNS, HTTPS, Nutanix API, backup traffic, NFS, iSCSI — so you understand what your network is doing.

DNS is the first step in almost every network conversation. Overwatch captures and analyzes DNS query patterns, resolution times, and failure rates to surface issues that affect everything downstream.
Don't just see what's happening now — replay what happened yesterday, last week, or last month. Historical flow data lets you investigate incidents after the fact and establish behavioral baselines.
Flow analysis isn't just for performance — it's a critical security and compliance tool.
Identify VMs communicating with unexpected external IPs, unusual ports, or geographic regions outside your normal traffic patterns.
Monitor lateral traffic between VMs within your clusters. Detect unusual internal communication patterns that could indicate compromise or misconfiguration.
Historical flow data provides documented evidence of network segmentation, access patterns, and communication paths for compliance audits.
A customer reported intermittent slowness on their production VMs every night between 1-4 AM.
Flow analysis revealed their backup proxy VM was saturating the 10GbE link to the storage tier during nightly backup windows, throttling production VM storage I/O.
Moved backup traffic to a dedicated network path. Production latency returned to baseline.
Overwatch flagged a development VM sending 2GB/day to an external IP in a region with no business presence.
Flow analysis revealed a developer had installed an unapproved monitoring agent that was streaming telemetry to a third-party SaaS tool — violating the organization's data residency policy.
Agent removed, firewall rule added, policy documentation updated.
Read the deeper guides on IPFIX, east-west traffic, and adjacent infrastructure context before you design your flow-analysis workflow.
IPFIX becomes most useful when it shows who is talking to whom, what changed, and how those traffic shifts line up with Nutanix performance symptoms.
IPFIX is not just a network feature. In Nutanix environments it can radically shorten the time between a vague symptom and a credible explanation.
Infrastructure teams lose time when hardware and network evidence live in separate products. Correlating Redfish and SNMP signals closes that gap.
Deploy Overwatch and have full IPFIX flow visibility within hours of configuration.

Network Visibility That Actually Helps

Packet counters tell you nothing about what's happening on your network. Overwatch shows you the actual conversations — which VMs are talking to which services, over which ports, and how traffic patterns change over time.

What You See With Flow Analysis
Dynamic Network Constellation

Visualize your entire network topology as an interactive constellation map. Every VM, every IP, every connection — color-coded by region, sized by traffic volume, and filterable by service, protocol, or time range.

Region-aware coloring: US, EU, Asia, SA traffic at a glance
Click any node to drill into its connections and traffic patterns

Automatic service identification and port mapping
Sankey diagram visualization: client → service → server
Traffic volume and direction for every service path

DNS Monitoring & Analysis

DNS query volume and response time tracking
Failed resolution detection and alerting
DNS server health and availability monitoring

Historical Flow Analysis

Full flow retention for historical investigation

Analysis & Correlation
ML analysis detects unusual traffic patterns, identifies top talkers, and correlates network events with compute and storage metrics.

Network Flow as a Security Signal

Flow analysis isn't just for performance — it's a critical security and compliance tool.

Unauthorized Connection Detection

Identify VMs communicating with unexpected external IPs, unusual ports, or geographic regions outside your normal traffic patterns.

East-West Traffic Visibility

Monitor lateral traffic between VMs within your clusters. Detect unusual internal communication patterns that could indicate compromise or misconfiguration.

Audit & Compliance Evidence

Historical flow data provides documented evidence of network segmentation, access patterns, and communication paths for compliance audits.

The Noisy Backup Discovery
Scenario:

A customer reported intermittent slowness on their production VMs every night between 1-4 AM.

Finding:

Flow analysis revealed their backup proxy VM was saturating the 10GbE link to the storage tier during nightly backup windows, throttling production VM storage I/O.

Resolution:

Moved backup traffic to a dedicated network path. Production latency returned to baseline.

Detection time with Overwatch: 4 minutes. Previous manual investigation: 3 weeks.
The Unauthorized Egress Alert
Scenario:

Overwatch flagged a development VM sending 2GB/day to an external IP in a region with no business presence.

Finding:

Flow analysis revealed a developer had installed an unapproved monitoring agent that was streaming telemetry to a third-party SaaS tool — violating the organization's data residency policy.

Resolution:

Agent removed, firewall rule added, policy documentation updated.

Detection: Automatic via anomaly detection. Without flow analysis: potentially never discovered.

paras: Datadog dashboards and alerts with real Nutanix context behind them

Overwatch bridges Nutanix and Datadog so teams can work in Datadog without flattening Prism, AHV, storage, and network telemetry into generic signals. You keep the Datadog workflow you already use, but feed it cleaner and more useful Nutanix-aware data.
The value is not just data transport. It is sending Nutanix data into Datadog in a way the operations team can actually use.
Send Nutanix management-plane signals into Datadog with cluster-aware context, not just generic API polling.
Track hosts, VMs, storage behavior, and hypervisor posture in a Datadog workflow that still understands Nutanix layers.
Expose capacity posture, growth trends, and predictive planning signals in Datadog dashboards and monitors.
Forward enriched telemetry into Datadog so monitors have more operational context and fewer noisy one-off signals.

Datadog is most useful when the data arriving there is already grouped around the operating questions teams ask: which cluster is drifting, which host is under pressure, which container is slowing down, and which alerts actually describe the same incident.

The point is not to mirror the appliance into Datadog one-to-one. The point is to keep deep troubleshooting local, then forward the right operational signals into the platform the wider team already uses.

Overwatch is available on the Datadog Marketplace as a native integration path. That matters for teams that want Nutanix-aware telemetry inside Datadog without creating a side project around API polling, manual dashboard authoring, and custom tagging strategy.

Marketplace delivery also aligns procurement with the Datadog workflows many organizations already use. Instead of introducing a one-off exporter or a custom integration that only one engineer understands, the team can deploy a supported Nutanix-aware path that already knows how to represent clusters, CVMs, containers, and project context.

The setup stays simple because Overwatch handles collection and forwarding. There is no Datadog Agent on AHV hosts, no SNMP workaround for Prism data, and no need to maintain a custom polling pipeline just to get Nutanix metrics into your Datadog org.

paras: Nutanix-aware Grafana dashboards without rebuilding the data pipeline yourself
Overwatch helps teams use Grafana as the visualization layer for Nutanix monitoring while preserving the cluster, Prism, AHV, storage, and network relationships that matter during real operations.
Grafana is powerful when the source telemetry is modeled well. Overwatch makes the Nutanix side of that equation much stronger.
Populate Grafana with Nutanix-aware dashboards for clusters, workloads, storage, network flow, and operational health.
Bring Prism Central and Prism Element signals into Grafana without forcing operations teams to rebuild every panel from scratch.
Trace workload behavior across hosts, VMs, storage, and hypervisor layers with dashboards that preserve Nutanix context.
Use Grafana for long-horizon trend views, runway analysis, and executive reporting on cluster posture and growth.

Overwatch includes Grafana internally as part of the appliance, so teams start with more than 50 pre-built dashboards locally on day one. For many environments that is enough: the appliance becomes the local operational front door and nobody has to build dashboards from raw Prism API output.

For teams already invested in Grafana, Overwatch can also act as the Nutanix-aware data source layer behind your existing dashboards. It forwards curated metrics to self-hosted Grafana or Grafana Cloud while keeping raw telemetry and troubleshooting depth local.

That gives teams two workable operating modes from the same license: use the dashboards already built into the appliance, or keep Grafana as the front door while Overwatch handles the Nutanix-aware collection, enrichment, and retention behind it.
The value is not just connectivity. It is starting from an operational model that already knows which Nutanix views matter.
Overwatch connects directly to your Grafana instance and keeps the full data path on-prem. Local dashboards remain available even if the wider analytics tier is offline.
Overwatch forwards curated metrics to Grafana Cloud while keeping raw telemetry, high-resolution detail, and troubleshooting history on the appliance.

You can query the Prism API from Grafana with a custom data source and start building dashboards by hand. The tradeoff is that you begin with empty panels, generic API payloads, and no Nutanix-aware alerting or retention strategy.

Overwatch handles the API polling, preserves the Nutanix hierarchy, correlates with hardware, backup, and network data, and ships with dashboards and baselines already in place. Grafana then becomes the presentation layer for a complete operational model instead of a blank canvas connected to raw JSON.

Teams that start with direct Prism queries usually spend weeks rebuilding cluster, host, storage, and VM dashboards that Overwatch already provides. The appliance shortens that path by shipping the data model, dashboards, and Nutanix-specific context before Grafana ever has to render the first panel.
The strongest Grafana rollout starts with the cluster, AHV, predictive analytics, and network pages feeding the visualization layer.

What Overwatch adds to Grafana

Grafana is powerful when the source telemetry is modeled well. Overwatch makes the Nutanix side of that equation much stronger.

Dashboard-ready Nutanix views

Populate Grafana with Nutanix-aware dashboards for clusters, workloads, storage, network flow, and operational health.

Prism-aware management visibility

Bring Prism Central and Prism Element signals into Grafana without forcing operations teams to rebuild every panel from scratch.

AHV and VM correlation

Trace workload behavior across hosts, VMs, storage, and hypervisor layers with dashboards that preserve Nutanix context.

Capacity and historical trend analysis

Use Grafana for long-horizon trend views, runway analysis, and executive reporting on cluster posture and growth.

How Overwatch works with Grafana

Overwatch includes Grafana internally as part of the appliance, so teams start with more than 50 pre-built dashboards locally on day one. For many environments that is enough: the appliance becomes the local operational front door and nobody has to build dashboards from raw Prism API output.

For teams already invested in Grafana, Overwatch can also act as the Nutanix-aware data source layer behind your existing dashboards. It forwards curated metrics to self-hosted Grafana or Grafana Cloud while keeping raw telemetry and troubleshooting depth local.

That gives teams two workable operating modes from the same license: use the dashboards already built into the appliance, or keep Grafana as the front door while Overwatch handles the Nutanix-aware collection, enrichment, and retention behind it.

Pre-built Nutanix dashboards

The value is not just connectivity. It is starting from an operational model that already knows which Nutanix views matter.

Cluster Overview: health scores, capacity, IOPS, and latency across all clusters
Host Performance: per-host CPU, memory, CVM health, and NIC throughput
VM Analytics: top consumers, outliers, and snapshot sprawl
Storage Deep Dive: container IOPS, latency, capacity trending, and oplog health
Network Flow: east-west traffic maps, top talkers, and DNS health
Backup Compliance: HYCU job status, RPO adherence, and protection gaps
Executive Summary: health and capacity reporting for non-technical stakeholders
Self-hosted Grafana vs Grafana Cloud
Self-hosted Grafana

Overwatch connects directly to your Grafana instance and keeps the full data path on-prem. Local dashboards remain available even if the wider analytics tier is offline.

Grafana Cloud

Overwatch forwards curated metrics to Grafana Cloud while keeping raw telemetry, high-resolution detail, and troubleshooting history on the appliance.

Why not just connect Grafana directly to Prism?

You can query the Prism API from Grafana with a custom data source and start building dashboards by hand. The tradeoff is that you begin with empty panels, generic API payloads, and no Nutanix-aware alerting or retention strategy.

Overwatch handles the API polling, preserves the Nutanix hierarchy, correlates with hardware, backup, and network data, and ships with dashboards and baselines already in place. Grafana then becomes the presentation layer for a complete operational model instead of a blank canvas connected to raw JSON.

Teams that start with direct Prism queries usually spend weeks rebuilding cluster, host, storage, and VM dashboards that Overwatch already provides. The appliance shortens that path by shipping the data model, dashboards, and Nutanix-specific context before Grafana ever has to render the first panel.

• Keep Grafana as the team’s front door while Overwatch handles Nutanix data collection and enrichment behind the scenes.
• Use Nutanix-specific signals in Grafana alerts and dashboards instead of relying on generic exporters alone.
• Combine Nutanix performance, network flow, and capacity data into one Grafana operating picture.

/prism-central-monitoring/.title: Prism Central Monitoring | Logic Insight
h1s: Prism Central Monitoring

headings.text: What teams need from Prism Central monitoring
What Overwatch collects from Prism Central
Why generic tools miss Prism Central context
Why teams pair Prism Central with Overwatch
See Prism Central monitoring in Overwatch

paras: Multi-cluster operational visibility without losing Nutanix context

Overwatch helps platform teams monitor Prism Central as the operational control plane for Nutanix. See clusters, alerts, categories, protection policies, projects, and administrative context in one place, then push cleaner telemetry to Datadog or Grafana.
Generic infrastructure tools can ingest alerts, but they rarely understand the management model Nutanix teams operate through every day.
Track health, capacity, performance, and operational status across every cluster registered to Prism Central.
Collect Prism Central alerts, enrich them with infrastructure context, and forward cleaner signals to Datadog or Grafana.
Understand protection policies, project-level usage, category alignment, and operational guardrails from one management surface.
Expose management-plane activity, access patterns, and change context for teams supporting shared Nutanix environments.

Prism Central is not just another API endpoint. It is the management hierarchy for the entire Nutanix estate. Overwatch preserves that hierarchy and makes it usable in dashboards, alerts, reports, and downstream integrations.

That matters because Prism Central is where the management context lives. A VM does not just belong to a host and a cluster. It may also belong to a project, inherit a category, sit under a protection policy, and carry business meaning that the infrastructure team needs during an incident. Overwatch keeps that context attached to the signal instead of asking the operator to go hunt for it after the alert already fired.

It also means Prism Central data becomes more than a summary counter. Alert history is consumed as a real event feed. Protection policy compliance is treated as an operational state, not a one-time configuration check. Licensing posture, version drift, and administrative changes stay visible enough that platform teams can plan, review, and escalate from the same operating surface.
Generic monitoring platforms can query the Prism Central API, but they usually flatten the results into metrics and events that lose the hierarchy Nutanix teams actually operate through.

A protection policy covering 200 VMs in the Production category fails silently. Prism Central shows the drift in its protection view, but a generic tool that only pulled host metrics will not expose that backup gap until restore time. Overwatch keeps the protection status, project context, category membership, and cluster association together.

Prism Central manages multiple Prism Element clusters. Overwatch keeps that multi-cluster topology intact so teams can compare health, capacity, and risk across the estate without opening each cluster individually.
Compare headroom, alert load, and growth rate across every registered cluster from one view.
Keep a unified event timeline so upgrades, snapshots, migrations, and alerts line up chronologically.

headings.text: Network monitoring for every device on the wire.
Network monitoring lives in too many places
Everything a serious NMS does — in the same appliance as Nutanix
Drill into any device for the full picture

See SNMP monitoring in your environment

paras: Auto-discover switches, firewalls, and routers across every vendor in your fleet. Get port-level traffic, LLDP topology, VLAN inspection, and config backup from one Overwatch appliance — no separate NMS required.

Most teams stitch together a polling tool, a topology mapper, a trap collector, a config backup utility, and vendor-specific dashboards. None of them talk to each other, and the network team becomes the integration team.
Different tools for polling, topology, traps, and config. Each has its own UI, alerts, and learning curve.
Cisco, Arista, Juniper, and Palo Alto each ship their own monitoring suite. None of them cover the others.
Network alerts fire in isolation from the cluster, hardware, and backup signals that explain the impact.
Overwatch ships with a full SNMP monitoring stack built on top of the same platform you use for cluster, hardware, and backup observability.
Sweep subnets and identify devices automatically. Match vendors, classify roles, and start polling without manual onboarding.
LLDP and CDP neighbor discovery builds a live network topology. See what is connected to what across the entire fleet.
Per-port traffic, errors, drops, and saturation trends. Spot saturated uplinks and underused capacity at a glance.
Read and inspect VLAN membership, ARP tables, and forwarding databases for any device in the fleet.
Receive SNMPv1/v2c/v3 traps with rule evaluation, dry-run testing, and unified alert routing.
SSH/Telnet config backup with versioning and diff. Cross-vendor config sync with dry-run validation.
Click any device to get health metrics, port-level traffic, sensor data, system info, alerts, logs, and configuration in one place. No tab-switching across vendor consoles.
Every interface gets traffic charts with ingress and egress overlays, error counters, and operational state. Hover any time range to compare patterns across days, weeks, or months.

Overwatch reads LLDP and CDP from every monitored device and builds a continuously updated map of what is physically connected to what. See spine-leaf paths, identify orphan ports, and trace connectivity issues in seconds.
Overwatch ships with vendor profiles for every major network platform. Adding a new device family is a YAML file — not a custom code drop or vendor support ticket.
When SNMP data lives next to cluster, hardware, backup, and flow telemetry, the network team and the infrastructure team finally see the same picture during incidents.
Stop splitting visibility across separate polling, topology, config, and trap utilities. One platform covers all of it.
Overwatch covers both network behavior and the device configurations that often explain it. No separate config tool required.
New device families are added through YAML profiles. No vendor lock-in, no waiting for product updates.
Deploy Overwatch and start collecting from your switches, firewalls, and routers alongside your Nutanix metrics — within minutes of deployment.
